title: Toxicological profile of anti-diabetic drugs and emerging measures to reduce the toxic burden

Title: Toxicological profile of anti-diabetic drugs and emerging measures to reduce the toxic burden

Type: research_article

[SECTION: INTRODUCTION]
According to the International Diabetes Federation Atlas, about 463 million people (9.3% of the global population) were living with either type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) or type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) in 2019. This figure is expected to hit 578 million (10.2%) by 2030 and 700 million (10.9%) by 2045  [1] . This growing incidence of diabetes mellitus (DM) prevalence is one of the most serious public health concerns of the 21st century and drastic measures are being sought to halt this increase  [2] . Individuals with T1DM do not produce enough insulin, and this constitutes about 5%–10% of all cases of DM  [3] . There is an autoimmune-mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells in T1DM, resulting in hypoinuslinemia due to a decrease in functional beta cell mass  [4] . T1DM is often seen in children and young people. The prevalence of T2DM is much higher (90% - 95%) than T1DM and over 40% of T2DM patients reside in China, India and the United States of America  [5] ,  [6] . In individuals with T2DM, there is a decline in insulin production coupled with insulin resistance. Other factors such as obesity, diet, alcohol intake, cigarette and tobacco smoking, physical inactivity and genetic susceptibility all play significant roles in the development of T2DM  [6] .
The most common and clinically relevant symptoms of DM include polydipsia, polyuria, weight loss, depression, diabetic ketoacidosis, and in extreme cases, coma  [7] ,  [8] . Majority of these symptoms are often not fatal, but uncontrolled DM may lead to permanent injuries such as treatment-resistant foot ulcers and amputations  [9] . Uncontrolled DM causes damage and/or failure of different organs such as the kidneys (nephropathy), eyes (retinopathy), liver (metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis) and limbs (neuropathy)  [10] ,  [11] . Chronic hyperglycemia activates different molecular pathways implicated in DM. These pathways promote increased production of reactive oxygen species (ROS; a destructive mediator of cell and tissue injury), inflammation and inflammatory stress mediators, metabolic stress, insulin resistance, free fatty acids and pancreatic beta cell death  [12] . These products cumulatively lead to beta-cell dysfunction by inducing endoplasmic reticulum stress, which activates the apoptotic unfolded protein response (UPR) pathways. Recent shreds of evidence highlight lipotoxicity and glucotoxicity - hallmarks of chronic hyperglycemia - as the ultimate cause of beta-cell damage and dysfunction  [12] . The decrease in the metabolic response of insulin-responsive cells to insulin or impaired response to circulating insulin is known as insulin resistance. Dysregulation of insulin signaling and the insulin receptors by non-hormonal bodies such as lipids and counter-regulatory hormones such as glucagon play a key role in the development of insulin resistance  [13] . Three main extra-pancreatic insulin-sensitive organs: skeletal muscles, adipose tissue and liver, play essential roles in meeting and regulating the body’s energy demands  [14] . Defective action of insulin in these tissues often precedes the development of systemic insulin resistance, thus leading to T2DM.
A plethora of approaches has been employed in the management of DM, ranging from non-pharmacological to pharmacological interventions. Non-pharmacological approaches employed include reduced intake of carbohydrates, lipids and alcohol, weight loss in overweight and obese patients and smoking cessation. Pharmacological agents used in the management of DM target several pathways involved in disease development and progression such as glucotoxicity, insulin resistance, gastric emptying, beta-fatty acid oxidation and increased lipotoxicity.

Literature search strategy:
This narrative review was conducted using a structured literature search to identify relevant studies examining the toxicological profiles of anti-diabetic drugs and strategies to mitigate their associated toxic burden. Electronic databases including PubMed/MEDLINE, Web of Science, and Google Scholar were systematically searched.
The search covered articles published from January 2000 to January 2026, with particular emphasis on more recent studies to ensure contemporary relevance. Search terms used alone and in combination included: “diabetes mellitus,” “anti-diabetic drugs,” “hypoglycemic agents,” “toxicity,” “adverse effects,” “organ toxicity,” “cardiovascular safety,” “renal toxicity,” “hepatic toxicity,” “mitochondrial dysfunction,” and “pharmacogenomics.” Drug class–specific terms (e.g., insulin analogs, sulfonylureas, metformin, thiazolidinediones, GLP-1 receptor agonists, DPP-4 inhibitors, SGLT2 inhibitors, and alpha-glucosidase inhibitors) were also included.
Eligible publications comprised randomized controlled trials, observational studies, cohort and case–control studies, systematic reviews, meta-analyses, mechanistic experimental studies, regulatory safety communications, and post-marketing surveillance reports. Case reports were included selectively where they provided mechanistic insight or highlighted rare but clinically significant toxicities. Conference abstracts without full data, non-English publications, and studies lacking sufficient methodological detail were excluded.
Data extraction focused on type of anti-diabetic agent, reported toxicities, affected organs or systems, proposed molecular or pathophysiological mechanisms, incidence rates or relative risks where available, and clinical implications. Evidence from randomized trials was prioritized for safety signal strength, while observational and real-world studies were used to contextualize long-term and rare adverse effects.

Classes of anti-diabetic drugs and their toxic effects:
A host of drugs are currently in use for the management of DM. These drugs are broadly classified as insulin, insulin secretagogues (biguanides and meglitinides), insulin sensitizers (such as sulfonylurea and thiazolidinediones), alpha-glucosidase inhibitors, peptide analogs (including glucagon-like peptide analogs, dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitors and amylin analogs) and sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 inhibitors. Additionally, antihypertensives and lipid-lowering agents are used in combination with anti-diabetic agents in patients with comorbidities (hypertension and/or dyslipidemia). This review will focus on tissue- and organ-specific damage associated with these pharmacological agents.

Insulin and insulin analogs:
Insulin is a hormone produced by β-cells of pancreatic Islet of Langerhans, and is responsible for blood glucose regulation. Impairment of insulin production and/or secretion leads to the development of DM  [15] . Regular human insulin (RHI) and insulin analogs have been the mainstay in the pharmacological management of all T1DM patients and a majority of individuals with T2DM. RHI is structurally identical to endogenously secreted insulin, but is characterized by a delayed onset and a longer duration of action, which can lead to early post-prandial hyperglycemia and late postprandial hypoglycemia  [16] . Despite these benefits, RHI has not been able to offer optimal glucose control over a 24-hour period in diabetic patients  [17] .
Insulin analogs were developed to have a better pharmacokinetic profile than RHI. These analogs have additional advantages over RHI such as less fear of dose adjustments, user-friendly injection devices and improvement of treatment satisfaction and adherence due to fewer injections per day  [18] . Insulin analogs are classified into rapid-, short-, intermediate- and long-acting products according to their pharmacokinetic properties  [19] . Currently approved rapid-acting analogs include Lispro, Aspart and Glulisine; short-acting analogs include Humalog and NovoRapid; intermediate-acting analogs include Neutral Protamine Hagedorn (NPH), long-acting analogs include glargine and detemir; and ultra-long-acting analogs include degludec. These analogs are approved for the management of DM globally. A systematic review and meta-analysis of nine randomized controlled trials (RCTs) involving 2693 participants with T1DM, comparing short-acting insulin analogues to RHI over a mean follow up of 37 weeks, demonstrated a small but statistically significant improvement in glycemic control in favor of insulin analogues, with HbA1c reduced by 0.15% (P < 0.00001), though the clinical relevance of this difference is questionable  [16] . Insulin Icodec, a first-of-its-kind insulin analog for once-weekly administration in humans, is currently being developed and tested  [20] . A 26-week RCT comparing once-weekly insulin icodec to once-daily glargine U100 in 247 participants with T2DM inadequately controlled on oral anti-diabetic medications demonstrated that insulin icodec achieved comparable glycemic efficacy (HbA1c reductions of 1.33% vs 1.15% glargine, between-group difference = −0.18%, P = 0.08) and a similar safety profile to glargine U100  [21] , suggesting it may be viable as an alternative basal insulin option with the potential advantage of once-weekly dosing regimen.
Beyond its established glycemic benefits, insulin therapy is associated with a range of toxicities that can be broadly categorized into acute metabolic, local and immune-mediated, and chronic proliferative or organ-specific effects  [22] . Acute toxicities are primarily metabolic, with hypoglycemia being the most immediate and potentially life-threatening adverse effect of insulin therapy. Hypoglycemia may result from excessive dosing, missed meals, unplanned physical activity, or impaired drug clearance, particularly in individuals with renal insufficiency. The ensuing neuroglycopenic symptoms including confusion, seizures, and coma, reflect the brain’s absolute dependence on glucose for energy metabolism. Mechanistically, hypoglycemia-induced neuronal injury involves neuronal energy failure, excitotoxicity, and oxidative stress, ultimately leading to mitochondrial dysfunction and inflammatory activation in vulnerable brain regions  [23] .
At the molecular level, severe hypoglycaemia triggers a cascade of neuronal injury mediated by excitotoxicity, oxidative stress, and energy failure. Glucose deprivation leads to excessive extracellular glutamate accumulation and over-activation of N-methyl- D -aspartate (NMDA) receptors, resulting in pathological intracellular calcium influx, mitochondrial membrane potential disruption, and impaired ATP synthesis  [24] . This calcium overload drives a burst of reactive oxygen species that damages neuronal DNA and activates poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase-1 (PARP-1), which then depletes cellular NAD⁺ and ATP reserves, further compounding neuronal energy failure and driving programmed cell death, particularly in vulnerable hippocampal and cortical regions  [25] ,  [26] . Critically, this mechanistic cascade can persist and worsen even after blood glucose normalization, as calcium dysregulation and PARP-1 activation continue beyond the hypoglycemic episode itself, contributing to the cognitive impairment observed in patients with a history of recurrent severe hypoglycemia  [25] .
A systematic review and meta-analysis of 24 RCTs involving 4740 adults with T2DM, comparing long-acting insulin analogues (insulin glargine and insulin detemir) to NPH insulin over a 24 week to 5 year duration demonstrated that both long acting insulin analogues resulted in fewer participants experiencing hypoglycemia compared to NPH insulin  [27] . Both insulin glargine and insulin detemir appear to offer advantages over NPH insulin in reducing hypoglycemia, though the strength of evidence varies. Insulin glargine showed a 32% reduction in severe hypoglycemia and 25% reduction in serious hypoglycemia compared to NPH, but neither result reached statistical significance (P = 0.06 and P = 0.13 respectively)  [27] . Insulin detemir showed an even larger directional benefit (55% reduction in severe hypoglycemia), though also not statistically significant (P = 0.11). Notably, insulin detemir demonstrated a statistically significant 84% reduction in serious hypoglycemia (Peto OR 0.16, P = 0.007), though this finding was based on only 5 trials and should be interpreted with caution. Treatment with insulin glargine and detemir also reduced the incidence of confirmed hypoglycemia and confirmed nocturnal hypoglycemia  [27] . The low to very low certainty of evidence across these trials, however, limits the strength of these conclusions.
In addition to hypoglycemia, acute insulin toxicity may precipitate hypokalemia through stimulation of Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, which drives potassium from the extracellular space into cells, causing a rapid dose-dependent fall in serum potassium levels without altering total body potassium  [28] . This electrolyte disturbance carries significant cardiac risk. Hypokalemia reduces Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity, promotes intracellular calcium overload via Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger dysregulation, and activates Ca²⁺/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (CaMKII), collectively prolonging ventricular depolarization and predisposing to life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias  [29] . This risk is particularly pronounced in patients with underlying cardiac disease, those concurrently using beta-agonists or diuretics, and in the context of diabetic ketoacidosis management where total body potassium depletion may be masked by normal or elevated baseline serum levels  [30] . Routine potassium monitoring and prophylactic supplementation are therefore essential components of safe insulin therapy  [30] .
Local adverse effects of insulin therapy include lipohypertrophy and lipoatrophy, which results from repeated injections into the same subcutaneous sites and localized anabolic or inflammatory responses to insulin or its analogues. Lipohypertrophy can impair insulin absorption and glycemic control, while lipoatrophy, although less frequent with modern recombinant preparations, remains a manifestation of localized immune-mediated fat necrosis. Immune-mediated complications of insulin therapy include the development of insulin antibodies, hypersensitivity reactions, and rarely, insulin autoimmune syndrome. Insulin autoimmune syndrome is characterized by the formation of autoantibodies against endogenous or exogenous insulin, leading to unpredictable episodes of hypoglycemia  [31] . With modern analogues and human recombinant preparations, the frequency of immune-related reactions has markedly declined, though cross-reactivity with additives such as protamine or zinc can still occur  [32] .
Chronic insulin exposure has been associated with mitogenic and proliferative signaling, especially through hybrid insulin/IGF-1 receptor pathways. These effects promote cellular proliferation and anti-apoptotic signaling, potentially playing key roles in tumorigenesis in predisposed tissues. Epidemiological studies have suggested possible associations between long-term use of insulin analog (e.g., insulin glargine) and malignancy risk. However, the data remain inconsistent after adjustment for confounders such as hyperinsulinemia and obesity  [33] . Molecular investigations further indicate that structural modifications in certain analogues, notably insulin glargine, enhance IGF-1 receptor affinity and downstream mitogenic potency, raising concerns about proliferative safety in chronic users  [34] .
One major concern threatening the long-term use of RHI and certain insulin analogs, such as glargine, is their reported mitogenic or carcinogenic potential  [35] . The insulin signaling pathway and insulin-growth factor (IGF) system are well-established in the promotion of cell growth, proliferation and tumorigenesis mediated through the actions of hormones (IGF-1, IGF-2) and receptors (insulin receptors; IR, IGF-1R and IGF-2R)  [36] . It has been demonstrated that, compared to RHI, some insulin analogs such as glargine and detemir exhibit higher insulin-growth factor binding affinity  in vitro  and in animal studies  [27] . In a retrospective study, insulin administration before the diagnosis of breast cancer was associated with poor survival rates in patients with breast cancer  [37] . Joenputri and Siregar  [38]  further reported an increased risk of breast cancer development in diabetic patients treated with insulin glargine. The use of inhaled insulin has been associated with an increased risk of developing lung malignancies and tumorigenesis in susceptible individuals with T2DM  [39] . Liver, pancreatic, bladder and neuroendocrine cancer risks were 20% higher in diabetic patients on RHI as reported by Vicentini  et al .  [40] , along with an increased occurrence of pancreatic neoplasm and upregulation of  Igf-1r ,  Insr , and  Igf-1  genes in the colon and liver of diabetic rats  [41] ,  [42] . Zhang and colleagues  [43]  revealed that insulin glargine can promote thyroid cell proliferation and tumour cell migration at supraphysiological, but not physiological, concentrations. In contrast, other studies have reported no significant increase in cancer risks in diabetic patients treated with either RHI or insulin analogs  [44] ,  [45] . The association between insulin analogs and carcinogenesis has been proven by some researchers as non-existent, but rather as a result of hyperinsulinemia  [46] ,  [47] . Besides their mitogenic potential, adverse effects encountered by patients using insulin and insulin analogs include hypoglycemia, weight gain, erythema, edema, lipodystrophy and hypokalemia  [48] ,  [49] . Individuals with asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease using inhaled insulin may experience cough, throat pain and acute bronchospasm  [50] .
Insulin therapy influences cardiovascular homeostasis through vasodilatory, pro-oxidant, and pro-inflammatory pathways. Acute insulin infusion exerts vasodilatory effects via nitric oxide release, whereas chronic hyperinsulinemia may potentiate endothelial dysfunction, promote smooth muscle proliferation, and stimulate sympathetic overactivity - mechanisms linked to atherogenesis and hypertension  [51] . Renal and hepatic effects have also been observed, particularly in individuals with preexisting organ impairment. Chronic hyperinsulinemia may contribute to renal hypertrophy and glomerulosclerosis, while hepatic insulin resistance promotes steatosis and dyslipidemia.
Insulin pharmacodynamics can be altered by concomitant drug use and dietary composition. Beta-blockers, salicylates, ACE inhibitors, and alcohol can potentiate hypoglycemia, whereas corticosteroids, thiazides, and sympathomimetics antagonize insulin action. Emerging evidence indicates that high-fructose diets and branched-chain amino acid supplementation may amplify the lipogenic and inflammatory effects of insulin, exacerbating metabolic stress and mitochondrial dysfunction  [52] . Individual variability in insulin toxicity may arise from polymorphisms in insulin receptor signaling genes, antioxidant enzymes, or immune regulators. Epigenetic changes driven by chronic hyperinsulinemia - such as DNA methylation of insulin receptor substrate genes - may perpetuate insulin resistance and metabolic toxicity across generations  [53] . Insulin secretagogues:
Insulin secretagogues stimulate insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells, and are widely used in the pharmacological management of T2DM. These oral hypoglycemic agents primarily act on ATP-sensitive potassium (K ATP ) channels, which play a major role in controlling β-cell membrane potential. Inhibition of K ATP  channels by insulin secretagogues results in the depolarization of β-cell membranes which subsequently triggers the opening of voltage-gated Ca 2 +  channels. This leads to an influx and elevation of intracellular Ca 2+ , stimulating exocytosis of insulin-containing secretory granules, and thereby increasing circulating levels of insulin  [54] . There are two main classes of insulin secretagogues currently used clinically: sulfonylureas (SUs) and meglitinides. SUs and meglitinides have varying affinity for the sulfonylurea receptor-1 (SUR1) subunit of the K ATP , and this is responsible for the subtle pharmacodynamic differences such as onset, duration and overall clinical effect between both classes of insulin secretagogues  [55] .

Sulfonylureas:
Sulfonylureas (SUs) are a class of oral insulin secretagogues used in the pharmacological management of T2DM, often in combination with metformin and other anti-diabetic agents. There are two generations of SU, namely, first-generation SUs (e.g. acetohexamide, tolazamide, chlorpropamide, tolbutamide), second-generation SUs (e.g. gliclazide, glyburide/glibenclamide, glipizide, glimepiride). The risk of hypoglycemia is reduced with the newer generation of SU anti-diabetic agents, and the selection of a specific SU should be patient-based  [56] . Longer-acting SUs such as chlorpropamide and glyburide are not recommended for use in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) and the elderly due to an increased risk of developing hypoglycemia  [57] ,  [58] . Common adverse effects of second-generation SUs include headache, dizziness, paresthesia, abdominal discomfort and nausea. Weight gain is another adverse effect associated with the use of SUs.
Hypoglycemia is the most common adverse effect associated with intentional or accidental overdose of SUs, and may manifest between 8 and 12 h after ingestion  [58] . Some symptoms of hypoglycemia include muscle tremors, confusion, hunger, irritability and tachycardia. In extreme cases, hypoglycemia may lead to loss of consciousness, seizure, coma, or death  [59] . A five-year retrospective study by Quadrani and associates  [60]  on the oral use of sulfonylureas in children reported that 27% experienced hypoglycemia, defined as blood glucose levels below 60 mg/dL. In children, hypoglycemia typically develops later, between 11 and 45 h after taking SUs, whereas in adults, it tends to occur sooner. The incidence of hypoglycemia increases when SUs are taken together with medications such as sulfonamides, gemfibrozil or warfarin, as these drugs can displace SUs from their plasma albumin binding sites  [61]  SU-induced hypoglycemia is managed by administering oral glucose or glucose-rich products such as soft drinks in conscious patients. In hospitalized patients, intravenous dextrose is the preferred treatment. Other agents used to reverse SU-induced hypoglycemia include glucagon, diazoxide and octreotide  [62] . The clinical magnitude of sulfonylurea-associated hypoglycemia is well-documented across large observational studies. Among elderly patients aged 70 years and above with T2DM, sulfonylurea use was associated with an adjusted HR of 2.52 (95% CI 2.23–2.84) for severe hypoglycemia compared to non-exposed subjects, with a 10-year absolute risk 7.7–8.6% higher across age groups  [50] . Compared directly to metformin, sulfonylurea use was associated with an adjusted HR of 2.50 (95% CI 2.23–2.82) for hypoglycemia, with glibenclamide carrying the highest individual agent risk (HR 7.48, 95% CI 4.89–11.44) and risk further amplified in patients with eGFR below 30 mL/min/1.73 m² (HR 4.96, 95% CI 3.76–6.55)  [63] . These findings underscore the importance of agent selection within the sulfonylurea class, with shorter-acting, SUR1-selective agents such as gliclazide preferred in elderly patients and those with renal impairment.
Despite the widespread use of SUs in the pharmacological management of DM, current evidence highlights increased cardiovascular toxicity associated with their use. A population-based cohort study conducted in Hong Kong revealed that SU administration was linked to a higher risk of ventricular arrhythmias or sudden cardiac death compared to metformin use in T2DM patients, regardless of disease severity  [64] . For individual SUs, glipizide has been associated with an increased risk of death from cardiovascular diseases, while glimepiride has been linked with an increased risk of a stroke  [65] . However, glimepiride has been shown to offer a degree of cardioprotection by lowering the threshold required for cardiac ischemic preconditioning  [66] . In contrast, glibenclamide has been associated with increased cardiotoxicity, likely due to its high affinity for K ATP  channels. Glibenclamide also increases the permeability of the inner mitochondrial membrane to chloride channels, further disrupting the mitochondrial energy homeostasis  [67] . Gliclazide and glimeperide have been shown to be associated with a lower risk of cardiovascular-related mortality compared to glibenclamide  [68] .
It must be noted that the degree of cardiotoxicity associated which each SU is linked to the presence of K ATP  channels in extra-pancreatic tissues, with varying affinities for these channels  [69] . A number of these extra-pancreatic tissues contain an alternative type of SUR subunit, specifically SUR2A in heart and SUR2B in smooth muscle  [70] . For example, tolbutamide, gliclazide and glimepiride exhibit high affinity for pancreatic beta-cell K ATP  channels but do not block the cardiac or smooth muscle types K ATP  channels with the same affinity  [71] . Glibenclamide, in contrast, blocks both SUR1 and SUR2 subunits with comparable affinity  [72] . This pharmacodynamics profile partly explains the adverse effect associated with some SUs currently in use.
